# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Differentiate between Immersion and Presence in the context of AR/VR systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Immersion | Presence |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Nature** | Objective (system property) | Subjective (user feeling) |
| **Measured by** | Technical specs (FOV, resolution, refresh rate) | Self-report scales (PQ, Slater's PI/PSI) |
| **Depends on** | Hardware, display fidelity | User perception, content quality |
| **Example** | A 4K, 120Hz HMD is highly immersive | A user forgets the real world and "feels" inside the game |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A highly immersive system may not always induce high presence (e.g., a boring VR experience on top-spec hardware). Presence requires both *technical immersion* AND *content plausibility* (Slater & Wilbur, 1997).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**List the four key activities of the User-Centered Design (UCD) process as per ISO 9241-210.**

**Model Answer:**

The four key activities are:

1. **Use Context of Use** — Understand who the users are, what tasks they perform, and in what environment.
2. **Specify User Requirements** — Translate research findings into concrete design requirements and user goals.
3. **Produce Design Solutions** — Generate prototypes, wireframes, and spatial UI designs that meet the requirements.
4. **Evaluate Against Requirements** — Test the solutions with real users using usability methods (heuristics, SUS, PQ, etc.) and iterate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** **U-S-P-E** → **U**se, **S**pecify, **P**roduce, **E**valuate. The cycle is iterative — Evaluation feeds back to Use Context.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark questions, students typically lose marks for (1) confusing *persona* with *stereotype* or *demographic profile*, (2) omitting the *emotional curve* in journey maps, and (3) failing to mention **both** system immersion AND content plausibility when discussing presence. **Always structure your answer with headings, tables, and diagrams** — KTU examiners award 1-2 marks for presentation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **User Persona** in detail. Describe the components of a persona document and discuss the difference between a persona, a proto-persona, and an anti-persona.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the **5 layers of a User Journey Map**. Plot an **emotional curve** for the journey of a first-time user of a VR fitness app, identifying at least 3 pain points and 3 design opportunities.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Persona Components & Distinctions [7 Marks]:**

A **User Persona** (Cooper, 1999) is a research-grounded, fictional archetype that represents a cluster of real users sharing similar **goals, behaviors, and pain points** — *not* a demographic stereotype.

**Components of a Persona Document (1 mark per major component, ~4 marks):**

| # | Component | Purpose | Example |
|---|-----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Header (Name, Photo, Tag)** | Humanize the persona | "Riya, 28, First-time VR User" |
| 2 | **Bio & Background** | Context, tech-savviness | "Marketing professional, uses smartphone daily, never owned a VR headset" |
| 3 | **Goals** | What success looks like for the user | "Lose 5kg in 3 months while enjoying workouts" |
| 4 | **Frustrations / Pain Points** | What blocks the user | "Gym intimidation, time constraints, motion sickness fears" |
| 5 | **Behaviors & Habits** | Patterns of action | "Works out at home, watches YouTube fitness, prefers evening sessions" |
| 6 | **Quote** | Memorable essence | *"I want to feel like I'm in a real class, not just staring at a screen."* |

**Distinctions (~3 marks):**

| Aspect | Proto-Persona | Persona | Anti-Persona |
|--------|---------------|---------|--------------|
| **Data Source** | Internal team assumptions | Real user research | Explicit exclusion criteria |
| **Validation** | None | Validated against data | Validated against team agreement |
| **Purpose** | Early ideation, alignment | Design & dev decisions | Defines "who is this NOT for" |
| **Risk** | May embed bias | Low (data-backed) | May exclude valid edge cases |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** A persona is **behavioral and goal-based**, not just demographic. Saying "Riya is 28, female" is *not* a persona — that is a demographic tag. The **goals, frustrations, and behaviors** are what make a persona useful for design.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Journey Map & Emotional Curve [7 Marks]:**

A **User Journey Map** (Richardson, 2010) is a visual artifact that captures the **end-to-end experience** of a specific user (persona) trying to accomplish a specific goal.

**5 Layers of a User Journey Map (1.5 marks for the diagram + 1.5 marks for explanation):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1: Persona - WHO] --> L2[Layer 2: Scenario - WHAT, WHEN, CONTEXT]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Phases - Awareness, Consideration, Onboarding, Usage, Loyalty]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Actions Thoughts Emotions]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Pain Points and Opportunities]
```

**Emotional Curve for First-Time VR Fitness App User (3 marks):**

| Phase | Action | Thought | Emotion (1-5) |
|-------|--------|---------|----------------|
| **Awareness** | Sees ad on Instagram | "VR fitness sounds cool, but I'm scared of motion sickness" | 3 (Curious but anxious) |
| **Consideration** | Reads reviews, watches demos | "Mixed reviews — some say it's great, some quit due to nausea" | 2.5 (Skeptical) |
| **Onboarding** | Sets up headset, struggles with straps | "This is harder than I thought, straps hurt my face" | 1.5 (Frustrated — **Valley of Despair**) |
| **First Workout** | Tries boxing class | "Wow, this is amazing — feels like a real class!" | 4.5 (Delighted) |
| **Loyalty** | Uses 3x per week, invites friends | "I look forward to my VR workouts" | 5 (Advocate) |

**Visualization (textual):**

$$
\text{Emotion: 3} \rightarrow 2.5 \rightarrow 1.5 \rightarrow 4.5 \rightarrow 5
$$
$$
\text{Stage: Awareness} \rightarrow \text{Consideration} \rightarrow \text{Onboarding} \rightarrow \text{Usage} \rightarrow \text{Loyalty}
$$

**3 Pain Points (0.5 each):**
1. Onboarding complexity (strap adjustment, account setup).
2. Fear of motion sickness (no comfort options in app).
3. Unclear pricing model (one user got charged after a "free trial").

**3 Design Opportunities (0.5 each):**
1. Add **guided onboarding with video** for strap adjustment.
2. Offer **comfort modes** (vignetting, teleport-only locomotion, 30-min limit for first sessions).
3. Add **clear pricing transparency** before checkout.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Contextual Inquiry (CI)** method in detail. Explain Beyer & Holtzblatt's **Master-Apprentice Model** and list the four principles. Discuss **two specific modifications** required when applying CI to AR/VR systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Spatial Design Principles** for AR/VR with examples. Describe the **Arm's Length Rule**, the **Comfortable FOV Cone**, and **Proxemic Zones (Hall, 1966)** as applied to spatial UI placement.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Contextual Inquiry & AR/VR Adaptations [7 Marks]:**

**Contextual Inquiry (CI) — Beyer & Holtzblatt, 1998:**
A field research method where the researcher observes users in their **natural environment** while performing real tasks, interrupting only to ask "Why?" to surface **tacit knowledge** (knowledge users cannot articulate).

**Master-Apprentice Model (1.5 marks):**
- **User = Master** of their own work; possesses deep, embodied, often unconscious knowledge.
- **Researcher = Apprentice** who must learn by watching, asking, and interpreting.
- The relationship is **collaborative** — researcher shares interpretations back with the user for confirmation.

**Four Principles of CI (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| # | Principle | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Context** | Observe users where they actually work, not in a lab. |
| 2 | **Partnership** | Researcher and user share the inquiry; researcher is not detached. |
| 3 | **Interpretation** | Researcher proposes models of user work; user confirms or corrects. |
| 4 | **Focus** | Researcher steers the session toward the project's specific questions. |

**Two AR/VR-Specific Modifications (3 marks — 1.5 each):**

**Modification 1 — Spatial & Embodied Observation:**
Standard CI focuses on screen-based tasks. In AR/VR, the researcher must additionally observe:
- **Head movement patterns** (does the user look around naturally?)
- **Hand gestures and reach** (does the user fatigue from "gorilla arm"?)
- **Proxemics** (how close does the user get to virtual objects?)
- **Postural shifts** (do they lean, crouch, or step back?)

*Example:* A researcher studying a VR cooking app notes that users instinctively lean forward when "stirring" — this reveals an opportunity to add a haptic stir-feedback at a closer depth.

**Modification 2 — Comfort & Presence Capture:**
- **Pre-session SSQ (Simulator Sickness Questionnaire)** to measure baseline nausea.
- **Mid-session observation** for fatigue cues (rubbing temples, removing headset).
- **Post-session SSQ** to quantify sickness increase.
- **Presence measurement** via Witmer & Singer's PQ.
- **Sensor logs:** Eye-tracking heatmaps, motion trajectories, grip pressure on controllers.

*Example:* A researcher testing a VR rollercoaster app notes 3 out of 5 users show > 5-point SSQ increase — the app needs **comfort vignetting** and **reduced velocity** for first-time users.

---

**Model Solution to (b) — Spatial Design Principles [7 Marks]:**

**Spatial Design Principles (2 marks for overview):**
Spatial design principles are guidelines for positioning, scaling, and organizing digital content in 3D space to optimize **perception, comfort, and task performance** in AR/VR systems.

**1. Arm's Length Rule (2 marks):**

$$
d_{\text{optimal UI}} \approx 0.5 \text{ m to } 2.0 \text{ m from user}
$$

- **Why 0.5 m minimum?** At closer distances, the eyes experience **vergence-accommodation conflict** — the eyes converge on a near object but focus (accommodate) at a farther virtual distance, causing eye strain and headache.
- **Why 2.0 m maximum?** Beyond arm's reach, users cannot comfortably interact via hand tracking; they must rely on raycasting, which loses tactile fidelity.
- **Application:** Place primary action buttons and toolbars in this zone; push ambient/dashboards farther (3-5 m) and personal notifications closer (0.3-0.5 m).

**2. Comfortable FOV Cone (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Comfortable FOV} \approx 60° \text{ around central gaze vector}
$$

- Content placed within this cone is **viewable without neck strain**.
- Content outside the cone (e.g., behind the head, in extreme periphery) requires **head or eye movement** to view, causing fatigue in long sessions.
- **Application (Apple Vision Pro HIG):** Place **critical alerts** (incoming call, navigation prompt) within $30°$ of central gaze; place **secondary information** (battery, time) at the periphery (up to $60°$).

**3. Proxemic Zones — Hall, 1966 (3 marks):**

| Zone | Distance | Spatial UI Application | Example |
|------|----------|------------------------|---------|
| **Intimate** | $< 0.5$ m | Personal, sensitive data | Health metrics, private messages |
| **Personal** | $0.5 - 1.2$ m | Primary controls, active widgets | Main menu, tool palette |
| **Social** | $1.2 - 3.6$ m | Shared information, dashboards | Team stats in multi-user VR |
| **Public** | $> 3.6$ m | Ambient cues, wayfinding | Building maps, environmental info |

**Why proxemics matter in AR/VR:**
- Users **instinctively feel uncomfortable** if UI is placed in the intimate zone (it feels intrusive).
- Multi-user VR needs **social-zone placement** for shared content to avoid "personal space violations."
- **Application:** In a collaborative VR meeting, place the shared whiteboard in the social zone (2-3 m) and personal notes in the personal zone (1 m directly in front of each user).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — High-Density Revision Checklist**

### UCD Foundations
- [x] UCD has **4 activities** per ISO 9241-210: **Use → Specify → Produce → Evaluate** (cycle).
- [x] UCD reduces risk, aligns with Agile, and is empirically grounded.
- [x] Always use **methodological triangulation** (qualitative + quantitative).

### User Research
- [x] Qualitative methods: Contextual Inquiry, Interviews, Diary Studies, Ethnography, Focus Groups.
- [x] Quantitative methods: Surveys, Card Sorting, A/B Testing, Analytics, Heuristic Evaluation.
- [x] Nielsen's saturation point: **5-8 users** for qualitative research.

### Personas
- [x] Persona = **behavioral and goal-based** archetype (Cooper, 1999), NOT a demographic stereotype.
- [x] Components: Header, Bio, Goals, Frustrations, Behaviors, Quote.
- [x] Proto-persona (assumption-based) vs. Persona (data-based) vs. Anti-persona (exclusion-based).
- [x] Empathy Map = 4 quadrants: Says, Does, Thinks, Feels.

### User Journey Maps
- [x] 5 layers: Persona, Scenario, Phases, Actions/Thoughts/Emotions, Pain Points/Opportunities.
- [x] **Emotional curve** is the KTU exam-favorite visual — always plot it.
- [x] Identify the "**Valley of Despair**" (lowest emotion point) for design focus.

### Contextual Inquiry for AR/VR
- [x] Beyer & Holtzblatt (1998) — Master-Apprentice Model.
- [x] 4 principles: Context, Partnership, Interpretation, Focus.
- [x] AR/VR additions: spatial/embodied observation + comfort/presence capture (SSQ, PQ).

### Designing for AR/VR
- [x] VR = synthetic world; AR = layer over real world.
- [x] Never replicate 2D UI in 3D — design **natively** for depth.
- [x] 3D UI Loop: **Selection → Manipulation → Result** (Bowman et al., 2004).

### Spatial Design Principles
- [x] **Arm's Length Rule**: UI at **0.5 - 2.0 m**.
- [x] **Comfortable FOV Cone**: ~**60°** around central gaze.
- [x] **Proxemic Zones (Hall, 1966)**: Intimate < 0.5 m, Personal 0.5-1.2 m, Social 1.2-3.6 m, Public > 3.6 m.
- [x] Use Z-axis deliberately: closer = more important/actionable.

### Immersion & Presence
- [x] **Immersion** = objective system property; **Presence** = subjective user response.
- [x] **Slater & Wilbur (1997)**: Place Illusion + Plausibility = Presence.
- [x] Witmer & Singer's **PQ (Presence Questionnaire)** — 19 items, 4 factors.
- [x] Cybersickness increases with **latency > 20 ms**, low refresh rate, and excessive movement.

### UI for AR/VR
- [x] WIMP (2D) vs. **VIMS** (Volumetric, Immersive, Manipulable, Spatial) paradigm.
- [x] Input modalities: Gaze + Dwell (Midas Touch risk), Gaze + Pinch, Hand Tracking, Controller Raycast, Voice, Haptic.
- [x] Apple Vision Pro HIG: critical UI in **30°**; secondary in **60°**; ambient farther.

### KTU 2024 Exam Strategy
- [x] Always include **diagrams/tables** in 14-mark answers (1-2 marks for presentation).
- [x] Always mention **both** system immersion AND content plausibility for presence questions.
- [x] Use ISO standards (9241-210) and seminal authors (Cooper, Beyer & Holtzblatt, Slater, Hall) for credibility.
- [x] Avoid confusing persona with stereotype; always tie it to **goals and behaviors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 carries **15-20% weightage** in the End Semester Exam. Expect **1 Part-A question (3 marks)** and **1 Part-B question (14 marks)** from this module. Master the diagrams in Section 4 and the tables in Section 2 — they are the most exam-rewarding artifacts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prototyping and Testing: Rapid Prototyping Techniques, Usability Testing Methods, Iterative Design and Feedback Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST865 - NEXT GENERATION INTERACTION DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prototyping and Testing: Rapid Prototyping Techniques, Usability Testing Methods, Iterative Design and Feedback Loops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Prototyping — The Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Prototyping** is the iterative process of creating preliminary versions (mock-ups, sketches, wireframes, or functional simulations) of an interactive system to explore ideas, test assumptions, validate user requirements, and uncover usability issues **before** committing to full-scale development.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Next Generation Interaction Design*, prototyping is positioned as the **bridge between ideation and implementation**. A prototype is an *artifact* (paper, digital, or hybrid) that externalizes a design idea so it can be inspected, manipulated, and critiqued.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 explicitly couples *Prototyping* with *Testing* and *Iterative Design* — emphasizing that no prototype is final; each cycle refines the system.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of prototyping like **building a scale model of a house before the real construction begins**. An architect never pours concrete on day one. They first create:

- A **2D blueprint** (≡ *low-fidelity wireframe*),
- A **foam-board scale model** (≡ *mid-fidelity prototype*),
- A **virtual 3D walkthrough** (≡ *high-fidelity interactive prototype*).

The model lets the client *feel* the door placement, window height, and traffic flow — catching errors when they cost **rupees, not lakhs**. The same logic governs software/HCI prototyping.

## 1.2 Rapid Prototyping — KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Prototyping (RP)** refers to techniques that allow designers to produce *quick, inexpensive, and disposable* representations of a system within hours or days (rather than weeks), so that feedback can be gathered and design decisions can be validated in compressed design cycles.

The word **Rapid** is the key differentiator — it emphasizes *speed of iteration* over *polish of output*.

### Intuition
Imagine writing an essay. You could spend 6 hours perfecting paragraph 1, or write the entire essay in 30 minutes and revise it 5 times. The second approach usually yields a better final result. **Rapid prototyping = "fail fast, learn faster."**

## 1.3 Usability Testing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Usability Testing** is an empirical, user-centered evaluation method in which representative users perform representative tasks with a prototype (or finished product) while observers watch, listen, and record metrics — to identify usability problems and gather qualitative/quantitative data.

Standard usability metrics (per ISO 9241-11 and Nielsen's framework):

- **Effectiveness** — Can the user complete the task? (*Task Success Rate*)
- **Efficiency** — How much effort/time/resources are required? (*Time on Task*)
- **Satisfaction** — How does the user feel? (*SUS Score*)

### Intuition
Imagine launching a new food delivery app. Before the big launch, you sit 8 customers in front of it and say: *"Order a biryani to MG Road."* If 5 out of 8 succeed, your **effectiveness is 62.5%**. If the average user takes 90 seconds, that is your **efficiency baseline**. If users groan or smile, that is your **satisfaction** signal.

## 1.4 Iterative Design — KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Iterative Design** is a cyclic design methodology in which the design is continuously refined through repeated cycles of *Design → Prototype → Test → Analyze → Refine*, with each iteration informed by empirical findings from the previous cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Iteration is **non-negotiable** in KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework for PECST865. The Course Outcome (CO) mapping for Module 2 requires students to *apply* iterative cycles, not merely describe them.

### Intuition
Think of **iterative design as sculpting a statue from marble**. The first chip of the chisel reveals the rough shape; each subsequent chip reveals more detail. The sculptor never "finishes" the statue in a single stroke — they iterate.

## 1.5 Feedback Loops — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Feedback Loop** in interaction design is a closed information channel through which evaluation data (from users, stakeholders, analytics, or heuristic review) flows back into the design process to drive the next iteration.

Two canonical types:

| Loop Type | Description | Cycle Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Feedback Loop** | Amplifies successful patterns (reinforces good design decisions) | Short / Continuous |
| **Negative Feedback Loop** | Corrects deviations (fixes usability problems) | Medium / Per iteration |

### Intuition
A thermostat in your home is a feedback loop. When room temperature drops, the heater turns on; when it hits the target, the heater turns off. Similarly, a usability test that reveals users cannot find the "Checkout" button **feeds back** into the design — the next iteration places the button prominently.

---

## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 of PECST865** is internally assessed and frequently examined (both as 3-mark definitions and 14-mark design questions). Examiners expect students to:
> 1. Classify prototyping techniques by fidelity.
> 2. Select appropriate usability methods for a given scenario.
> 3. Justify the choice of iteration count.
> 4. Diagram feedback loops.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Prototyping Techniques (Fidelity Pyramid)

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames rapid prototyping on a **fidelity spectrum**. Lower fidelity = cheaper, faster, throwaway; higher fidelity = expensive, slower, reusable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fidelity** in prototyping = *how closely the prototype resembles the final product in look, feel, and behavior.*

```
            ▲ HIGH FIDELITY (Code-Complete Prototype)
            │   - Pixel-accurate UI
            │   - Real data, real interactions
            │   - 20–40% of project cost
            │
            │ MID FIDELITY (Click-through Wireframe)
            │   - Grayscale, boxy layouts
            │   - Limited interactions
            │   - 5–10% of project cost
            │
            │ LOW FIDELITY (Sketch / Paper)
            │   - Hand-drawn boxes
            │   - No real interactions
            │   - < 1% of project cost
            ▼
```

### 2.1.1 Rapid Prototyping Techniques — Detailed Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each technique below is **explicitly listed** in the KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus. Memorize the *technique name + best-use case + cost* triplet.

| # | Technique | Fidelity | Tool Examples | Best Use Case | Cost / Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Paper Prototyping** | Low | Pen, paper, sticky notes | Early concept testing, brainstorm validation | < 1 hr |
| 2 | **Wireframing** | Low–Mid | Balsamiq, Adobe XD, Figma | Information architecture, layout decisions | 1–4 hrs |
| 3 | **Storyboarding** | Low | Sketch + captions | User journey mapping, scenario analysis | 2–3 hrs |
| 4 | **Wizard of Oz** | Mid (deceptive) | Human operator simulates backend | Testing natural-language interfaces, chatbots | 1–2 days |
| 5 | **Interactive Mock-ups** | Mid | Figma, Sketch, InVision | Navigation flow validation | 1–3 days |
| 6 | **Coded / Functional Prototype** | High | HTML/CSS/JS, React, Flutter | Engineering feasibility, performance testing | 1–3 weeks |
| 7 | **3D-Printed Prototype** | High (physical) | Blender + 3D printer | Tangible/IoT product ergonomics | 2–7 days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wizard of Oz technique (Klasnja, 2004 – referenced academically):** The user believes they are interacting with an autonomous system, but a hidden human operator drives the response. The name comes from the 1939 film where the Wizard was a man behind a curtain.

## 2.2 Usability Testing Methods — The KTU Matrix

### 2.2.1 Classification Axes

Usability tests are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Moderation:** Moderated vs. Unmoderated
2. **Location:** In-Person vs. Remote
3. **Measurement Type:** Qualitative vs. Quantitative

### 2.2.2 The Six Core Methods (KTU Exam-Favored)

| # | Method | Type | Participants | Output | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Think-Aloud Protocol** | Qualitative, Moderated | 5–8 users | Verbalized thoughts, pain points | Exploratory problem discovery |
| 2 | **Heuristic Evaluation** | Expert-based (not user) | 3–5 evaluators | List of violations vs. Nielsen's 10 heuristics | Cheap, fast pre-launch audit |
| 3 | **Cognitive Walkthrough** | Expert-based | 1–3 evaluators | Step-by-step task failures | Early-stage, novice-user apps |
| 4 | **A/B Testing** | Quantitative, Unmoderated | Large N (100s–1000s) | Conversion rate differences | Data-driven design decisions |
| 5 | **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | Quantitative, Survey-based | 10+ users | 0–100 usability score | Standardized benchmarking |
| 6 | **Eye-Tracking** | Quantitative + Qualitative | 10–20 users | Gaze plots, heatmaps | Visual hierarchy, ad effectiveness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics** (1994) — frequently tested in KTU as a 3-mark question:
> 1. Visibility of system status
> 2. Match between system and the real world
> 3. User control and freedom
> 4. Consistency and standards
> 5. Error prevention
> 6. Recognition rather than recall
> 7. Flexibility and efficiency of use
> 8. Aesthetic and minimalist design
> 9. Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors
> 10. Help and documentation

## 2.3 Iterative Design Models

### 2.3.1 Double Diamond Model (UK Design Council, 2005)

```
     Discover ─────►  Define ─────►  Develop ─────►  Deliver
        ◇                ◇                ◇                ◇
   (diverge:        (converge:        (diverge:        (converge:
   explore          synthesize        prototype        finalize
   the problem)     the problem       many             the solution)
                                      solutions)
```

- **Diamond 1** = Problem space
- **Diamond 2** = Solution space
- **Iteration** happens *within* the Develop–Deliver diamond.

### 2.3.2 Spiral Model (Boehm, 1986)

A risk-driven iterative model where each loop = 1 cycle of *Plan → Risk Analysis → Engineer → Evaluate*. Each spiral produces a more complete prototype.

### 2.3.3 ISO 9241-210 User-Centered Design (UCD) Cycle

The KTU syllabus treats UCD as the canonical iteration framework:

```
        Understand ────► Specify ────► Design ────► Evaluate
            ▲                                              │
            │                                              │
            └──────── Iterate (refine) ◄────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **4 main UCD activities** (ISO 9241-210): *Use Context, Requirements, Design Solutions, Evaluation*. Iteration = repeating these 4.

## 2.4 Feedback Loops in Detail

### 2.4.1 Don Norman's Two Feedback Loops

> [!NOTE]
> **Norman (1988)** describes two loops in his *Action Cycle*:
> - **Execution Loop (Gulfs of Execution):** Goal → Plan → Action
> - **Evaluation Loop (Gulfs of Evaluation):** Perception → Interpretation → Comparison with Goal
> **Feedback** closes the Evaluation loop, letting the user know *what happened* after their action.

### 2.4.2 User Feedback Channels in Modern HCI

| Channel | Latency | Data Type | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **In-app analytics** | Real-time | Quantitative | Click heatmaps, funnel drop-offs |
| **In-app surveys (NPS)** | Real-time | Mixed | "How was your experience?" pop-up |
| **Usability lab session** | Per-iteration | Qualitative | Think-aloud recording |
| **App store reviews** | Continuous | Qualitative | Public user sentiment |
| **A/B test result dashboards** | Days–Weeks | Quantitative | Variant B wins by 4.2% |
| **Customer support tickets** | Real-time | Qualitative | Recurring complaints |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **high-yield formula table** for solving KTU calculation-style questions on this topic. Memorize these equations; they appear almost every semester.

| # | Metric / Formula | LaTeX | Typical KTU Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Task Success Rate** | $\text{TSR} = \dfrac{S}{N} \times 100\%$ | "5 out of 8 users completed the task. Find TSR." |
| 2 | **Time-on-Task Mean** | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i$ | "Average task time across 10 users." |
| 3 | **Time-on-Task Std Dev** | $\sigma_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_i - \bar{T})^2}$ | Variance analysis |
| 4 | **Error Rate** | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{E}{N} \times 100\%$ | "Errors per user" |
| 5 | **Nielsen's Sample Size** (qualitative) | $N = 5$ (rule of thumb) | "Minimum users for usability test" |
| 6 | **Faulkner's 5-User Rule Refinement** | $N = ?$ — see Section 3 | Statistical derivation |
| 7 | **SUS Score Calculation** | $\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times \sum ( \text{odd} - 5 + 25 - \text{even} )$ — see Section 3 | "Compute SUS from 10-question survey" |
| 8 | **NPS Score** | $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ | "60 promoters, 25 detractors, 15 passives" |
| 9 | **Conversion Rate (A/B Test)** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Visitors}} \times 100\%$ | A/B question |
| 10 | **Effect Size (Cohen's d)** | $d = \dfrac{\vert \bar{x}_A - \bar{x}_B \vert}{\sigma_{\text{pooled}}}$ | "Is the difference between variants significant?" |
| 11 | **Confidence Interval (95%)** | $\text{CI} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$ | "95% CI on task time" |
| 12 | **Iteration Cost Ratio** | $\text{ICR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{later fix}}}{C_{\text{prototype fix}}}$ | "Cost of fixing in production vs. prototype" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Alert:** In all KTU LaTeX formulas, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ for absolute value — **never** the bare `|` character, as it breaks the markdown table parser.

## 2.6 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering Practice

- **Industry adoption:** Every Fortune 500 UX team (Google, Apple, Microsoft, TCS iON) uses *rapid prototyping* + *usability testing* + *iteration* as the standard product-development rhythm.
- **Cost savings (Boehm's Rule):** A defect fixed during prototyping costs **$1$ unit**, but the same defect fixed post-launch costs **$100$–$1000\times$ more**.
- **Regulatory compliance:** ISO 9241-210 mandates UCD for safety-critical systems (medical devices, aviation cockpits).
- **KTU CO mapping:** Module 2 contributes directly to **CO2: Apply user-centered design processes** in the PECST865 course outcome matrix.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section performs **exhaustive step-by-step derivations** of every quantitative formula in Section 2.5 that is likely to appear in a KTU examination. No step is skipped.

## 3.1 Task Success Rate (TSR) — Full Derivation

### Problem Statement
In a usability test, 8 users were asked to *"Reset a forgotten password"*. 6 succeeded without assistance, 1 succeeded with a hint, and 1 gave up.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Define total participants.**
> The total number of participants $N$ in the test is the number of users who attempted the task.
> $$N = 8$$

> **Step 2 — Define the success criterion.**
> A task is "successful" if the user reaches the goal state, **with or without hints** (lenient criterion), OR without hints (strict criterion). The KTU 2024 scheme accepts both — but the question must specify.
> For lenient criterion: $S_{\text{lenient}} = 6 + 1 = 7$.
> For strict criterion: $S_{\text{strict}} = 6$.

> **Step 3 — Apply the TSR formula.**
> $$\text{TSR} = \dfrac{S}{N} \times 100\%$$

> **Step 4 — Compute lenient TSR.**
> $$\text{TSR}_{\text{lenient}} = \dfrac{7}{8} \times 100\% = 87.5\%$$

> **Step 5 — Compute strict TSR.**
> $$\text{TSR}_{\text{strict}} = \dfrac{6}{8} \times 100\% = 75.0\%$$

> **Step 6 — KTU-style interpretation.**
> A TSR of 75% (strict) or 87.5% (lenient) is **below the KTU-acceptable industry threshold of 90%** for a critical task like password reset. The design should be iterated.

## 3.2 Time-on-Task — Mean and Standard Deviation

### Problem Statement
Five users attempted a checkout flow. Their task times (in seconds) are: $T = \{45, 60, 50, 55, 90\}$. Find the mean and standard deviation.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Write the data set.**
> $$T_i = \{45,\ 60,\ 50,\ 55,\ 90\}, \quad i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, \quad N = 5$$

> **Step 2 — Compute the sum.**
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{5} T_i = 45 + 60 + 50 + 55 + 90 = 300\ \text{seconds}$$

> **Step 3 — Compute the mean.**
> $$\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i = \dfrac{300}{5} = 60\ \text{seconds}$$

> **Step 4 — Compute deviations from the mean.**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> (T_1 - \bar{T})^2 &= (45 - 60)^2 = (-15)^2 = 225 \\
> (T_2 - \bar{T})^2 &= (60 - 60)^2 = 0^2 = 0 \\
> (T_3 - \bar{T})^2 &= (50 - 60)^2 = (-10)^2 = 100 \\
> (T_4 - \bar{T})^2 &= (55 - 60)^2 = (-5)^2 = 25 \\
> (T_5 - \bar{T})^2 &= (90 - 60)^2 = 30^2 = 900
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 5 — Sum the squared deviations.**
> $$\sum (T_i - \bar{T})^2 = 225 + 0 + 100 + 25 + 900 = 1250$$

> **Step 6 — Compute population standard deviation.**
> $$\sigma_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_i - \bar{T})^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1250}{5}} = \sqrt{250} \approx 15.81\ \text{seconds}$$

> **Step 7 — (Alternative) Sample standard deviation (Bessel's correction).**
> $$s_T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_i - \bar{T})^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1250}{4}} = \sqrt{312.5} \approx 17.68\ \text{seconds}$$

> **Step 8 — KTU interpretation.**
> The 5th user's time (90 s) is an **outlier** — it inflates $\sigma_T$. The median (55 s) is more robust. KTU examiners often award marks for identifying outliers explicitly.

## 3.3 Nielsen's Sample Size — Saturation Curve Derivation

### Problem Statement
Nielsen (1993) argued that **5 users are enough** to find ~85% of usability problems. Derive this.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Empirical observation.**
> Nielsen observed that across 11 different usability studies, the cumulative number of *new* problems found versus *users tested* followed a logarithmic curve. The cost of each additional user grows, while the marginal discovery of new problems decays.

> **Step 2 — Formulate the cumulative discovery function.**
> Let $D(N)$ = number of distinct usability problems found in a test with $N$ users.
> Empirical fit: $D(N) = D_{\max} \cdot (1 - e^{-kN})$, where $D_{\max}$ is the total problem count in the population, and $k$ is a discovery rate constant.

> **Step 3 — Use Nielsen's empirical constants.**
> For typical software: $D_{\max} \approx 31$ problems (his average across 11 studies), $k \approx 0.49$.

> **Step 4 — Compute $D(5)$.**
> $$D(5) = 31 \cdot (1 - e^{-0.49 \times 5}) = 31 \cdot (1 - e^{-2.45})$$
> $$e^{-2.45} \approx 0.0863$$
> $$D(5) = 31 \cdot (1 - 0.0863) = 31 \cdot 0.9137 \approx 28.32 \approx 28\ \text{problems}$$

> **Step 5 — Compute the discovery percentage.**
> $$\%D(5) = \dfrac{D(5)}{D_{\max}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{28.32}{31} \times 100\% \approx 91.4\%$$

> **Step 6 — KTU interpretation.**
> Although the textbook figure is **~85% at $N=5$** (Nielsen's original 1993 estimate), modern replications (e.g., Sauro & Lewis, 2012) suggest closer to 90%+ for software products. Either figure is **acceptable in KTU exams** if justified.

> **Step 7 — Practical takeaway.**
> Testing 15 users (3× the cost) only marginally improves coverage, since $D(15) = 31 \cdot (1 - e^{-7.35}) \approx 31 \cdot 0.9994 \approx 30.98$. Diminishing returns justify the small sample.

## 3.4 System Usability Scale (SUS) — Full Calculation

### Problem Statement
A 10-user usability test produced the following SUS ratings. Compute the SUS score. The 10 statements are scored 1–5 (1=Strongly Disagree, 5=Strongly Agree).

| User | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | Q7 | Q8 | Q9 | Q10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| 2 | 3 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 1 |
| 3 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| 4 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| 5 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| 6 | 3 | 3 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 2 |
| 7 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 4 | 1 |
| 8 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 |
| 9 | 4 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 4 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 4 | 2 |
| 10 | 4 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 5 | 1 | 4 | 1 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Recall the SUS scoring rule.**
> - **Odd-numbered questions (1, 3, 5, 7, 9):** contribution = $\text{score} - 1$
> - **Even-numbered questions (2, 4, 6, 8, 10):** contribution = $5 - \text{score}$
> - **Total per user** = sum of all 10 contributions, then **multiplied by 2.5** to get a 0–100 scale.
> $$\text{SUS}_{\text{user}} = 2.5 \times \sum_{i=1}^{10} c_i$$
> where $c_i$ is the contribution of question $i$.

> **Step 2 — Compute User 1's contributions.**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> c_1 &= 4 - 1 = 3 \\
> c_2 &= 5 - 2 = 3 \\
> c_3 &= 4 - 1 = 3 \\
> c_4 &= 5 - 1 = 4 \\
> c_5 &= 4 - 1 = 3 \\
> c_6 &= 5 - 2 = 3 \\
> c_7 &= 4 - 1 = 3 \\
> c_8 &= 5 - 2 = 3 \\
> c_9 &= 4 - 1 = 3 \\
> c_{10} &= 5 - 2 = 3
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 3 — Sum User 1's contributions.**
> $$\sum c_i = 3+3+3+4+3+3+3+3+3+3 = 31$$

> **Step 4 — Scale to 0–100 for User 1.**
> $$\text{SUS}_1 = 2.5 \times 31 = 77.5$$

> **Step 5 — Repeat for all 10 users** (compact table; KTU expects you to show this in the answer key).

| User | Odd Sum | Even Sum | $\sum c_i$ | SUS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3+3+3+3+3 = 15 | 3+4+3+3+3 = 16 | 31 | **77.5** |
| 2 | 2+2+2+3+3 = 12 | 2+3+3+4+4 = 16 | 28 | **70.0** |
| 3 | 4+4+3+4+4 = 19 | 4+4+4+4+4 = 20 | 39 | **97.5** |
| 4 | 3+3+3+3+3 = 15 | 3+3+3+3+3 = 15 | 30 | **75.0** |
| 5 | 3+4+3+4+4 = 18 | 4+4+4+4+4 = 20 | 38 | **95.0** |
| 6 | 2+3+3+2+2 = 12 | 2+3+3+3+3 = 14 | 26 | **65.0** |
| 7 | 3+3+4+3+3 = 16 | 3+4+4+4+4 = 19 | 35 | **87.5** |
| 8 | 4+3+3+3+4 = 17 | 4+3+3+4+4 = 18 | 35 | **87.5** |
| 9 | 3+2+3+2+3 = 13 | 3+3+3+3+3 = 15 | 28 | **70.0** |
| 10 | 3+4+4+4+3 = 18 | 4+4+4+4+4 = 20 | 38 | **95.0** |

> **Step 6 — Compute the average SUS score.**
> $$\bar{\text{SUS}} = \dfrac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10} \text{SUS}_i$$
> $$\sum \text{SUS}_i = 77.5 + 70.0 + 97.5 + 75.0 + 95.0 + 65.0 + 87.5 + 87.5 + 70.0 + 95.0 = 820$$
> $$\bar{\text{SUS}} = \dfrac{820}{10} = 82.0$$

> **Step 7 — Interpret using the SUS adjective scale (Bangor et al., 2009).**

| SUS Range | Adjective | Grade |
|---|---|---|
| $\geq 85$ | Excellent | A |
| $72 - 84$ | Good | B |
| $50 - 71$ | OK | C |
| $35 - 49$ | Poor | D |
| $< 35$ | Awful | F |

> Our score: $\bar{\text{SUS}} = 82.0$ → **"Good" (Grade B)** — within the industry-acceptable range ($\geq 68$). No major redesign needed, but refinements could push it into "Excellent."

## 3.5 A/B Test — Conversion Rate Comparison

### Problem Statement
An e-commerce site A/B tests a new "Buy Now" button. Variant A: 1,200 visitors, 60 purchases. Variant B: 1,250 visitors, 95 purchases. Determine if B is significantly better.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Compute conversion rates.**
> $$\text{CR}_A = \dfrac{60}{1200} \times 100\% = 5.00\%$$
> $$\text{CR}_B = \dfrac{95}{1250} \times 100\% = 7.60\%$$

> **Step 2 — Compute the difference.**
> $$\Delta \text{CR} = \text{CR}_B - \text{CR}_A = 7.60\% - 5.00\% = 2.60\ \text{percentage points}$$
> $$\text{Relative lift} = \dfrac{2.60}{5.00} \times 100\% = 52.0\%$$

> **Step 3 — Compute the pooled standard error** (two-proportion z-test).
> $$\hat{p} = \dfrac{60 + 95}{1200 + 1250} = \dfrac{155}{2450} \approx 0.0633$$
> $$SE = \sqrt{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p}) \left( \dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_B} \right)}$$
> $$SE = \sqrt{0.0633 \times 0.9367 \times \left( \dfrac{1}{1200} + \dfrac{1}{1250} \right)}$$
> $$SE = \sqrt{0.0593 \times 0.001633} = \sqrt{9.68 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.00984$$

> **Step 4 — Compute the z-statistic.**
> $$z = \dfrac{\text{CR}_B - \text{CR}_A}{SE} = \dfrac{0.0760 - 0.0500}{0.00984} = \dfrac{0.0260}{0.00984} \approx 2.64$$

> **Step 5 — Compare with the critical value.**
> For 95% confidence (two-tailed), $z_{\text{crit}} = 1.96$. Since $z = 2.64 > 1.96$, **reject $H_0$**. Variant B is significantly better at $p \approx 0.008$.

> **Step 6 — Compute the 95% confidence interval for $\Delta \text{CR}$.**
> $$\text{CI}_{95\%} = \Delta \text{CR} \pm 1.96 \cdot SE = 0.0260 \pm 1.96 \cdot 0.00984 = 0.0260 \pm 0.0193$$
> $$\text{CI}_{95\%} = (0.0067,\ 0.0453) = (0.67\%,\ 4.53\%)$$

> **Step 7 — KTU interpretation.**
> The new button is **statistically significantly better** with 95% confidence. The true conversion-rate lift lies between 0.67 and 4.53 percentage points. Recommend rolling out Variant B.

## 3.6 Net Promoter Score (NPS)

### Problem Statement
After release, 200 users responded to an NPS survey: 90 Promoters, 70 Passives, 40 Detractors.

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Compute the percentages.**
> $$\%\text{Promoters} = \dfrac{90}{200} \times 100\% = 45\%$$
> $$\%\text{Detractors} = \dfrac{40}{200} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

> **Step 2 — Apply the NPS formula.**
> $$\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors} = 45 - 20 = +25$$

> **Step 3 — Interpret.**
> NPS ranges from $-100$ to $+100$. A score of $+25$ is **"Good"** (industry benchmark: $> 0$ is acceptable, $> 30$ is excellent). The app has more advocates than critics.

## 3.7 Iterative Design Cost Ratio (Boehm's Curve)

### Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Boehm's empirical cost ratio.**
> A defect fixed at the **requirements** stage costs **$1$ unit**; the same defect at the **design** stage costs **$5$–$10$ units**; at **coding** it costs **$10$–$50$ units**; at **post-release** it costs **$100$–$1000$ units**.

> **Step 2 — Concrete numerical example.**
> Suppose a usability defect is discovered:
> - In paper prototype: $C_{\text{proto}} = \text{₹}500$ (designer time, 2 hours).
> - Same defect fixed in production: $C_{\text{prod}} = \text{₹}50{,}000$ (engineer time + customer support + patch release).

> **Step 3 — Compute the cost ratio.**
> $$\text{ICR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{prod}}}{C_{\text{proto}}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{500} = 100\times$$

> **Step 4 — KTU interpretation.**
> The **iteration pays for itself 100 times over** — a powerful justification for usability testing in the KTU 14-mark design questions.

## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Prototyping Technique

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Which prototyping technique would you use for [scenario]?"* The expected answer is a **justified choice**, not a list. Use the matrix below.

| Scenario | Best Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Validating a brand-new app concept with 3 stakeholders | **Paper Prototype** | Fast, cheap, no coding skill needed |
| Testing conversational AI with 5 users | **Wizard of Oz** | Backend not built yet; human simulates AI |
| Final-stage A/B test on a live website | **High-Fidelity Coded Prototype** | Need pixel-accurate, real-data behavior |
| Evaluating 3D-printed IoT device ergonomics | **3D-Printed Prototype** | Need tangible, physical form-factor |
| Early-stage nav flow with 8 users | **Low-Fi Wireframe (Balsamiq)** | Validates IA without color/style distractions |
| Safety-critical medical UI (ISO 62366) | **High-Fi Coded + Heuristic Eval** | Regulatory mandate |

## 3.9 Iterative Design Cycle — Step-by-Step Walkthrough

> **Step 1 — Understand the user.** Conduct interviews, field studies, persona creation.
> **Step 2 — Specify requirements.** Translate findings into functional + non-functional requirements.
> **Step 3 — Design solutions.** Sketch multiple alternatives.
> **Step 4 — Build prototype.** Select fidelity based on risk.
> **Step 5 — Evaluate.** Test with 5 users (Nielsen) or 1 expert (heuristic).
> **Step 6 — Analyze findings.** Cluster problems by severity (Critical / Major / Minor).
> **Step 7 — Iterate.** Return to Step 3 (or Step 2) with refined assumptions.
> **Step 8 — Stop criterion.** When (a) task success rate $\geq 90\%$, (b) SUS score $\geq 80$, and (c) no critical usability problems remain.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Question structure follows the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**:
> - Part A: 2 questions × 3 marks (short answers, 60–80 words).
> - Part B: Internal choice — pick **either** Question A **or** Question B (14 marks each, two 7-mark sub-parts).
> Each question is tagged with a simulated KTU past-year context.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q1.** List any **three Nielsen usability heuristics** and briefly explain how each can guide the design of a mobile banking app.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994) are widely applied in interaction design. For a mobile banking app, three especially relevant heuristics are:

1. **Visibility of System Status** — After a money transfer, the app should display a clear confirmation screen and push-notify "Transfer of ₹2,000 successful at 14:32." This reassures the user that the action took effect (addresses the Gulf of Evaluation).
2. **Error Prevention** — When entering a transfer amount, the app should pre-validate input (e.g., disallow letters, show a slider for amounts, and ask "Are you sure?" before confirming large transfers). This reduces costly banking mistakes.
3. **Help and Documentation** — Provide a contextual "?" icon next to fields like IFSC code, with a one-line tooltip. Novice users can complete tasks without leaving the app.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *list* the heuristics without explaining *how* they apply to the specific domain. Always tie the heuristic to the **given context** (here: mobile banking). 1 mark per heuristic + application = full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q2.** What is the **Wizard of Oz prototyping technique**? State one scenario where it is most useful and one limitation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Wizard of Oz technique is a rapid prototyping method in which the user interacts with a system they believe to be autonomous, but a hidden human operator manually generates the system's responses. The technique gets its name from the 1939 film *The Wizard of Oz*, in which the great "wizard" was revealed to be a man behind a curtain.

**Best-Use Scenario (1 mark):** It is most useful for testing **conversational AI / chatbot / voice interface** designs before the natural-language model has been built. For example, a designer can test user reactions to a "smart home assistant" by having a hidden operator type the assistant's replies.

**Limitation (1 mark):** The technique is **time-intensive for the operator** (each session requires manual response generation) and may not scale to large user studies. Additionally, the operator's response latency may distort the test results if it differs from the expected real system's response time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse Wizard of Oz with simple "mock-ups." The defining feature is the *hidden human operator*. Mention the word "operator" or "behind the curtain" to score the definition mark.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 will offer **two 14-mark questions**, and you must attempt **one** (i.e., internal choice). Both options are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q.A.** A startup wants to design a **food delivery app for a college campus in Kerala**. They have 3 months, a small team (1 designer + 2 developers), and a tight budget of ₹5,00,000.

**(a)** Propose a **complete rapid prototyping plan** for this project. Justify your choice of **prototyping techniques at each stage** and the **number of iterations**.

**(b)** Design a **usability testing plan** for the final prototype. Include: goals, tasks, participant recruitment, metrics, and the **stopping criterion** for the iterative loop.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Project Context Summary (½ mark):**
A 3-month, ₹5 Lakh budget, 3-person team requires a *lean* iteration strategy. The risk of over-investing in a high-fidelity prototype too early is high.

**Step 2 — Stage 1: Paper Prototyping (Week 1) — 1 mark**

- **Technique:** Hand-drawn paper prototypes on A4 sheets; sticky notes for navigation.
- **Justification:** Cost is negligible (₹0), iteration time is < 1 day. Stakeholders can react in real time. The college-student user base is casual and forgiving of rough artifacts.
- **Deliverable:** 5–8 paper screens representing the core flow: Login → Browse Restaurants → View Menu → Add to Cart → Checkout → Track Order.

**Step 3 — Stage 2: Low-Fidelity Wireframes (Week 2) — 1 mark**

- **Technique:** Balsamiq or Figma wireframes (grayscale, boxy).
- **Justification:** Wireframes test *information architecture* without the designer's aesthetic choices biasing the user. They are sharable, can be linked into a click-through demo, and are faster to revise than code.

**Step 4 — Stage 3: Mid-Fidelity Interactive Mockup (Week 3–4) — 1 mark**

- **Technique:** Figma with full component library, click-through navigation, and realistic (placeholder) data.
- **Justification:** Lets 5–8 users complete real tasks in a moderated test. Validates flow logic before code is written.

**Step 5 — Stage 4: Wizard of Oz for Chatbot (Week 5, optional) — 1 mark**

- **Technique:** A hidden operator simulates an in-app "chat with support" feature.
- **Justification:** The team has no time to build an NLP pipeline; WoZ lets them validate whether users want a chat feature at all.

**Step 6 — Stage 5: High-Fidelity Coded Prototype (Week 6–10) — 1 mark**

- **Technique:** React Native or Flutter, integrated with a real backend (Firebase).
- **Justification:** The two developers can build a working app iteratively, pushing updates to TestFlight/internal Play Store.

**Step 7 — Stage 6: Final Polish + Launch (Week 11–12) — ½ mark**

- **Technique:** Final UI/UX polish, performance testing, accessibility audit.
- **Justification:** Buffer for unexpected bugs; aligns with the 3-month deadline.

**Step 8 — Number of Iterations Justification — 1 mark:**
Following Nielsen's 5-user rule, each major prototype stage undergoes **2–3 iterations** of usability testing (≈ 6 total cycles). Empirical evidence shows diminishing returns beyond 5 users per cycle, so the team tests with 5 users per cycle × 6 cycles = 30 total user-test sessions — within the 3-month timeline.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Goals (1 mark):**

- **Primary:** Achieve a Task Success Rate ≥ 90% for ordering food end-to-end.
- **Secondary:** Achieve a SUS score ≥ 80 ("Good").
- **Tertiary:** Identify and fix all "Critical" usability issues before public launch.

**Step 2 — Tasks (1 mark):**
Design **5 representative tasks**:
1. Sign up with a new college email.
2. Search for "biryani" and filter by price.
3. Add 2 items from a restaurant to the cart.
4. Apply a coupon code and check out.
5. Track an active order to delivery.

**Step 3 — Participant Recruitment (1 mark):**

- **N = 8** college students (per Nielsen's 5-user rule, +3 for a 1st-year demographic).
- Recruited via flyers on campus; each participant receives a ₹100 food voucher.
- **Screening criteria:** Owns a smartphone, has used Zomato/Swiggy before.

**Step 4 — Metrics (2 marks):**

| Metric | Type | Tool | Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| Task Success Rate | Quantitative | Manual scoring | ≥ 90% |
| Time on Task | Quantitative | Stopwatch | ≤ 90 s for task 1 |
| Error Count | Quantitative | Manual log | ≤ 1 per task |
| SUS Score | Quantitative | Post-task survey | ≥ 80 |
| Verbal Pain Points | Qualitative | Think-aloud audio | ≤ 3 critical |

**Step 5 — Stopping Criterion for the Iterative Loop (2 marks):**
The team exits the iteration loop when **all three** of the following are true:
1. $\text{TSR} \geq 90\%$ across all 5 tasks.
2. $\bar{\text{SUS}} \geq 80$ (Bangor "Good" threshold).
3. No "Critical" or "Major" usability issues remain in the latest round.

If any criterion fails, the team returns to **Stage 5** of the prototyping plan (Coded Prototype), fixes the issues, and runs another 5-user test cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Q.A:**
> 1. **Don't** skip the *justification* of why a technique was chosen — the verb "propose" demands reasoning.
> 2. **Don't** write a generic "test with 100 users" answer. KTU expects **Nielsen's 5-user rule** explicitly.
> 3. **Don't** forget the **stopping criterion**. The KTU 2024 CO2 mapping rewards a clear exit condition for the loop.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q.B.** A hospital wants to deploy a **new Electronic Health Record (EHR) system** for 200 doctors and nurses. Existing systems are slow, and complaints are rising.

**(a)** Compare **three usability testing methods** that would be most appropriate for this project, highlighting their **strengths and weaknesses** in the medical context.

**(b)** A pilot test with **5 doctors** produced the following SUS scores: $\{65, 70, 72, 68, 75\}$. Compute the **mean SUS score** and decide whether the system is ready to deploy. Justify your decision with a **feedback loop design** for the next iteration.

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Context (½ mark):**
A medical EHR system is a *safety-critical* application. Errors can lead to patient harm, so the usability test must combine **expert review**, **user feedback**, and **quantitative metrics**.

**Step 2 — Method 1: Heuristic Evaluation (1.5 marks)**

- **Description:** 3–5 UX experts independently inspect the EHR against Nielsen's 10 heuristics. Each violation is assigned a severity rating (0 = cosmetic, 4 = catastrophic).
- **Strengths:** Cheap, fast (days not weeks), catches issues that users may not articulate. Excellent for *medical-context* because experts know HIPAA / ABDM / DISHA standards.
- **Weaknesses:** Expert bias; experts may not notice workflow issues that only emerge during real use. Does not measure real-world task success.
- **Verdict:** Use this **first**, as a low-cost pre-flight check.

**Step 3 — Method 2: Think-Aloud Moderated Testing with Doctors/Nurses (1.5 marks)**

- **Description:** 5 doctors and 5 nurses are given 5 representative tasks (e.g., "pull up patient X's last 3 lab reports and flag the abnormal one") and asked to verbalize thoughts while the researcher observes.
- **Strengths:** Captures *real workflow friction*; reveals *language* issues ("the EHR says 'encounter' but we call it a 'visit'"). Generates rich qualitative data.
- **Weaknesses:** Time-consuming; Hawthorne effect (users behave differently when observed); small sample may not cover all specialties.
- **Verdict:** Use this **second**, to validate the heuristic findings with real users.

**Step 4 — Method 3: Remote Unmoderated Task Testing with SUS (1.5 marks)**

- **Description:** A larger cohort (50+ clinicians) uses the EHR on their own devices, completes tasks, and submits SUS surveys + error reports through a tool like UserTesting or Maze.
- **Strengths:** Scales to many participants; lower cost per participant; measures SUS for benchmarking. Captures data from doctors in different shifts/wards.
- **Weaknesses:** Moderator cannot intervene when users get stuck; data quality varies. Cannot probe "why" issues.
- **Verdict:** Use this **third**, for statistical robustness.

**Step 5 — Synthesis (1 mark):**
A defense-in-depth approach: **Heuristic (expert) → Think-Aloud (qualitative) → Remote SUS (quantitative)**. The three methods together cover expert review, real-use observation, and statistical validation — minimizing blind spots.

---

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Data set (½ mark):**
$$\text{SUS} = \{65, 70, 72, 68, 75\}, \quad N = 5$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sum (1 mark):**
$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} \text{SUS}_i = 65 + 70 + 72 + 68 + 75 = 350$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean (1 mark):**
$$\bar{\text{SUS}} = \dfrac{350}{5} = 70.0$$

**Step 4 — Compute the standard deviation (1 mark):**
$$\begin{aligned}
(\text{SUS}_1 - \bar{x})^2 &= (65-70)^2 = 25 \\
(\text{SUS}_2 - \bar{x})^2 &= (70-70)^2 = 0 \\
(\text{SUS}_3 - \bar{x})^2 &= (72-70)^2 = 4 \\
(\text{SUS}_4 - \bar{x})^2 &= (68-70)^2 = 4 \\
(\text{SUS}_5 - \bar{x})^2 &= (75-70)^2 = 25
\end{aligned}$$
$$\sum (\text{SUS}_i - \bar{x})^2 = 25 + 0 + 4 + 4 + 25 = 58$$
$$s = \sqrt{\dfrac{58}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{14.5} \approx 3.81$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the mean (1 mark):**
Using the Bangor et al. (2009) adjective scale, a SUS score of **70.0** falls in the range **"OK" (Grade C, 50–71)**. It is **above the industry acceptability threshold of 68** (Lewis & Sauro, 2018), so the system is *acceptable* but **not yet "Good"**. It is **not ready to deploy to 200 users** without one more iteration.

**Step 6 — Feedback Loop Design for Next Iteration (1.5 marks):**

| Phase | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Diagnose** | Cluster SUS comments: which items scored lowest? | E.g., Q4 "I felt confident using the system" = 2.0 — confidence is low. |
| **2. Targeted Fix** | Improve training videos + in-app tooltips for low-confidence areas. | Updated prototype v2. |
| **3. Re-test** | Run another 5-user SUS test. | New SUS scores. |
| **4. Stop Criterion** | $\bar{\text{SUS}} \geq 80$ AND no SUS item < 3.0. | If met → deploy. Else → iterate. |

**Step 7 — Final Recommendation (½ mark):**
Suspend the rollout. Run **one more iteration** (estimated 2 weeks). Re-test. If the new $\bar{\text{SUS}} \geq 80$, deploy to 200 users with confidence.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Q.B:**
> 1. **Don't** confuse *population* $\sigma$ and *sample* $s$ — for $N=5$, KTU expects the **sample standard deviation** ($N-1$).
> 2. **Don't** say "deploy" just because SUS = 70 > 68. Always **state the stop criterion** and check against it.
> 3. **Don't** forget to design the **feedback loop**, not just compute the mean. The verb "design" in part (b) demands a process diagram or a loop description.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **high-density revision sheet** — re-read within 2 hours before the exam.

### A. Prototyping
- [ ] **Fidelity = how close the prototype looks/acts like the final product.**
- [ ] **Low-fi** (paper, sketch): < 1 hr, < ₹500, used in **early concept validation**.
- [ ] **Mid-fi** (wireframe, click-through mockup): 1–3 days, used in **IA and flow validation**.
- [ ] **High-fi** (coded, polished): 1–3 weeks, used in **final user testing**.
- [ ] **Wizard of Oz** = user thinks it's autonomous, hidden human operates the system. Used for **conversational AI**.
- [ ] **Storyboarding** = sketching a user journey with frames.
- [ ] **3D printing** = physical product prototype.

### B. Usability Testing
- [ ] **Effectiveness = Task Success Rate** ($\text{TSR} = S/N \times 100\%$); target $\geq 90\%$.
- [ ] **Efficiency = Time-on-Task** ($\bar{T} = \frac{1}{N}\sum T_i$); lower is better.
- [ ] **Satisfaction = SUS** (0–100 scale); target $\geq 80$.
- [ ] **Nielsen's 5-user rule**: $N = 5$ uncovers ~85–90% of problems.
- [ ] **Nielsen's 10 heuristics** — memorize all 10 by name.
- [ ] **Think-Aloud** = qualitative, moderated; users verbalize thoughts.
- [ ] **Heuristic Evaluation** = expert-based; 3–5 evaluators, severity 0–4.
- [ ] **A/B testing** = quantitative, unmoderated, large N; uses 2-proportion z-test.
- [ ] **Eye-tracking** = visual attention analysis; uses gaze plots + heatmaps.

### C. Iterative Design
- [ ] **UCD cycle** = Understand → Specify → Design → Evaluate → Iterate.
- [ ] **Double Diamond** = Discover/Define + Develop/Deliver.
- [ ] **Stop criteria**: TSR ≥ 90% **and** SUS ≥ 80 **and** no Critical issues.
- [ ] **Boehm's cost ratio**: post-release fix is 100–1000× costlier than prototype fix.
- [ ] **Norman** = Gulf of Execution (goal → action) and Gulf of Evaluation (action → perception).

### D. Feedback Loops
- [ ] **Positive feedback loop** = amplifies good design.
- [ ] **Negative feedback loop** = corrects problems.
- [ ] **Channels**: in-app analytics, NPS, app store reviews, support tickets, usability lab.
- [ ] **Closing the loop** = feeding test results back into the next design iteration.

### E. Formula Quick-Fire (Memorize!)
- [ ] $\text{TSR} = \dfrac{S}{N} \times 100\%$
- [ ] $\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum T_i$
- [ ] $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum (T_i - \bar{T})^2}$
- [ ] $\bar{\text{SUS}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum \text{SUS}_i$
- [ ] $\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$
- [ ] $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Visitors}} \times 100\%$
- [ ] $z = \dfrac{p_B - p_A}{SE}$, where $SE = \sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_B}\right)}$

### F. Examiner's Most-Loved Traps
- [ ] **Never** say "prototypes are the final product" — they are throwaway or evolving.
- [ ] **Never** list heuristics without applying them to the given context.
- [ ] **Never** propose testing 100 users — KTU rewards **Nielsen's 5-user rule**.
- [ ] **Always** state a **stopping criterion** in any iterative design question.
- [ ] **Always** justify the choice of prototyping technique with cost + time + risk.
- [ ] **Always** draw a feedback loop in any "design a loop" question — words alone lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gesture: Designing for Gesture-Based Interaction, Implementing Gesture Controls in AR/VR Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST865 - NEXT GENERATION INTERACTION DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gesture: Designing for Gesture-Based Interaction, Implementing Gesture Controls in AR/VR Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Next Generation Interaction Design** under the KTU 2024 scheme, a **gesture** is a *non-verbal, body-mediated communicative act* — typically performed using the hand, fingers, head, eyes, or full body — that is computationally interpreted by a sensing system to issue commands, manipulate virtual objects, or convey semantic intent within a Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) loop.

**Gesture-Based Interaction (GBI)** is the design discipline concerned with specifying, recognizing, and responding to such gestures in a way that feels natural, learnable, and efficient. The KTU 2024 syllabus frames gesture design around three orthogonal axes:

1. **Recognition axis** — how the system *senses* the gesture (vision, inertial, electromyography, capacitive).
2. **Semantics axis** — what the gesture *means* (manipulative, semaphoric, conversational, symbolic).
3. **Feedback axis** — how the system *responds* (visual, haptic, auditory, none).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *“A gesture is a body movement, often performed using the hand or head, whose spatial and temporal characteristics are captured, classified, and mapped by a digital system to a discrete or continuous control signal, forming the basis of a natural user interface.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are standing in front of a smart TV. Instead of searching for a remote, you simply raise your hand and swipe right — and the channel changes. You did not press a button. You did not speak. Your hand movement **was** the command.

Now imagine you are wearing a VR headset. To grab a virtual cube, you extend your hand, close your fingers around the shape, and "pull" it toward you. The system sees your hand, understands your intent, and the cube obeys.

That is **gesture-based interaction** in a nutshell: **your body becomes the controller.** The computer is no longer a separate box with a keyboard; it is an invisible partner that watches what you naturally do and reacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Mental Anchors for Students:**
> - **Watch** — sensors capture your motion.
> - **Understand** — algorithms classify what you meant.
> - **React** — the virtual world updates accordingly.
>
> This **Watch → Understand → React** triad is the conceptual spine of every gesture system you will ever design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Gesture Design

| Metric | Standard Value / Range | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame rate of tracking** | **30 – 120 Hz** | Below 30 Hz causes perceptual lag |
| **Latency budget** | **≤ 70 ms** | Higher values feel "unresponsive" |
| **Recognition accuracy** | **≥ 95%** | Industry acceptance threshold |
| **Degrees of Freedom (DoF)** | **6 DoF** for hands | 3 for position, 3 for rotation |
| **Gesture vocabulary size** | **8 – 20 gestures** | Beyond this, recall collapses |
| **Fitts's Law index of difficulty** | **ID = log₂(2D/W)** | Used to evaluate pointing gestures |

> [!NOTE]
> The **70 ms latency budget** is the single most-cited number in KTU board answers for AR/VR gesture control. It is the perceptual threshold below which users do not consciously notice input delay.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gesture Recognition Pipeline (Operational Breakdown)

Every gesture-based system — whether it is in a smartphone, a Meta Quest headset, or a Microsoft HoloLens — executes the same **five-stage pipeline**:

1. **Sensing / Capture**
   - *What happens:* Cameras (RGB, IR, depth), IMUs (Inertial Measurement Units), or EMG (electromyography) sensors record raw data of the user's body.
   - *Why:* Without raw motion data, there is nothing to interpret.
2. **Preprocessing**
   - *What happens:* Noise removal, background subtraction, normalization of hand pose, smoothing.
   - *Why:* Raw sensor data is noisy; preprocessing makes it usable.
3. **Feature Extraction**
   - *What happens:* The system extracts meaningful features such as fingertip coordinates, joint angles, velocity vectors, or skeletal pose.
   - *Why:* Features reduce dimensionality and make classification tractable.
4. **Classification / Recognition**
   - *What happens:* An algorithm (rule-based, ML, or deep learning) maps the features to a gesture label.
   - *Why:* This is the "understanding" step.
5. **Mapping & Feedback**
   - *What happens:* The recognized gesture is mapped to a UI action, and feedback (visual, haptic, auditory) is returned.
   - *Why:* Without feedback, the user cannot confirm their gesture was understood.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects students to name **all five stages** when asked to "explain the gesture recognition pipeline." Memorize the order: **Sense → Preprocess → Extract → Classify → Map/Feedback**.

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Gestures (Wobbrock / HCI Standard)

Following the seminal HCI taxonomy by Wobbrock et al. (2009), KTU 2024 recognizes **four gesture classes**:

| Gesture Class | Definition | AR/VR Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Manipulative** | Directly controls a virtual object's properties (position, rotation, scale) | Pinch to scale a 3D model |
| **Semaphoric** | Static posture or symbol that issues a discrete command | OK-sign hand pose to confirm |
| **Gestural (Iconic)** | Movement that traces a symbol or path in space | Drawing a check-mark in air to "approve" |
| **Conversational** | Used to communicate with another agent (human or AI) | Nodding head while speaking to a NPC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for Board Exam:** *“My Students Can Chat”* → **M**anipulative, **S**emaphoric, **C**onversational, **G**estural-iconic (alphabetically rearranged for the rhyme).

## 2.3 Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics Applied to Gesture Design

KTU examiners frequently test whether students can apply established usability principles to gesture interfaces. The key adaptations:

- **Visibility of system status** → show a real-time hand cursor / avatar hand in VR.
- **Match between system and real world** → gestures should mirror physical intuition.
- **User control and freedom** → provide an "undo" gesture (e.g., reverse swipe).
- **Consistency and standards** → a "swipe right" means the same thing everywhere.
- **Error prevention** → require confirmation for destructive gestures (long press).
- **Recognition rather than recall** → display the gesture menu, not a cheat sheet.
- **Flexibility and efficiency** → allow custom gestures for power users.
- **Aesthetic and minimalist design** → do not clutter the gesture vocabulary.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete set of formulas** you need for any numerical or design question on this topic. Print it; memorize it.

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's law (basis of IMU acceleration sensing) | N |
| 2 | $\omega = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}$ | Angular velocity (basis of rotational gesture detection) | rad/s |
| 3 | $v = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | Linear velocity of hand/finger | m/s |
| 4 | $D_{\text{Euclidean}} = \sqrt{(x_2-x_1)^2 + (y_2-y_1)^2 + (z_2-z_1)^2}$ | Distance between two hand pose keypoints | meters |
| 5 | $D_{\text{DTW}}(A,B) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(a_i - b_i)^2}$ | Dynamic Time Warping distance between gesture templates | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{ID} = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{2D}{W}\right)$ | Fitts's Law — Index of Difficulty for pointing gestures | bits |
| 7 | $T = a + b \cdot \text{ID}$ | Fitts's Law — Movement time | seconds |
| 8 | $P_{\text{accuracy}} = \dfrac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}$ | Recognition accuracy (used in gesture classifier evaluation) | ratio (0–1) |
| 9 | $P_{\text{precision}} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Recognition precision | ratio (0–1) |
| 10 | $P_{\text{recall}} = \dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Recognition recall (sensitivity) | ratio (0–1) |
| 11 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{precision}} \cdot P_{\text{recall}}}{P_{\text{precision}} + P_{\text{recall}}}$ | F1-score (harmonic mean) | ratio (0–1) |
| 12 | $L_{\text{perceived}} = L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{compute}} + L_{\text{render}}$ | Total perceived latency | ms |
| 13 | $\theta_{\text{joint}} = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\vert \vec{u} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v} \vert}\right)$ | Joint angle between two bone vectors | radians |
| 14 | $\text{DoF}_{\text{total}} = 6 \cdot n_{\text{rigid-bodies}}$ | Degrees of Freedom (each rigid body = 6) | scalar |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board answers, always state the **unit** next to a formula. Examiners specifically check for this; a correct formula without units is treated as a partial-credit answer.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gesture-based interaction is foundational in:

- **Healthcare** — touchless control of surgical displays in sterile ORs.
- **Automotive** — in-cabin gesture control (BMW, Mercedes gesture suites).
- **Industrial training** — AR-assisted assembly line guidance (Siemens, Boeing).
- **Accessibility** — sign language recognition for hearing-impaired HCI.
- **Gaming & Metaverse** — hand tracking in Meta Quest 3, Apple Vision Pro.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fitts's Law for Pointing Gestures (Board-Favorite)

Fitts's Law models the time a user takes to acquire (point at) a target. For a gesture interface, the target is typically a virtual button or object.

**Step 1: Define the parameters**

Let $D$ = distance from the hand's starting position to the target center.
Let $W$ = width of the target along the direction of motion.

**Step 2: Define the Index of Difficulty (ID)**

The Shannon formulation of Fitts's Law is the one KTU accepts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ID} &= \log_2 \!\left( \frac{2D}{W} \right) \quad \text{[bits]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The factor 2 ensures ID is non-negative even when $D < W$. The logarithm base 2 expresses the task in "bits of information."

**Step 3: Define the movement time**

Throughput experiments, the movement time $T$ is linearly related to ID:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= a + b \cdot \text{ID} \quad \text{[seconds]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* $a$ is the start/stop time (device-dependent), and $b$ is the slope — empirically around **100 ms/bit** for hand pointing.

**Step 4: Numerical example (KTU-style)**

Suppose a user must point at a virtual button of width $W = 4$ cm located $D = 20$ cm away. With $a = 50$ ms and $b = 100$ ms/bit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ID} &= \log_2\!\left( \frac{2 \times 20}{4} \right) \\
          &= \log_2\!\left( \frac{40}{4} \right) \\
          &= \log_2(10) \\
          &\approx 3.3219 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 50 \text{ ms} + 100 \text{ ms/bit} \times 3.3219 \text{ bits} \\
  &= 50 + 332.19 \\
  &= 382.19 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the user will take approximately **382 ms** to complete the pointing gesture.

**Step 5: Interpretation for AR/VR design**

To minimize $T$, the designer should either **reduce $D$** (place targets closer to the user's natural hand rest position) or **increase $W$** (make buttons larger). In VR, a button smaller than 1 cm at arm's length is essentially unclickable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Joint Angle from Bone Vectors (Skeleton-Based Gestures)

Modern hand-tracking (e.g., MediaPipe, Meta Quest) returns 21+ 3D keypoints. The joint angle is computed from two adjacent bone vectors.

**Step 1: Define bone vectors**

Let $P_0, P_1, P_2$ be three consecutive keypoints (e.g., wrist, index-knuckle, index-fingertip).
Define:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{u} &= P_1 - P_0 \quad \text{(bone 1)} \\
\vec{v} &= P_2 - P_1 \quad \text{(bone 2)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the dot product**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} &= u_x v_x + u_y v_y + u_z v_z \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute magnitudes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert \vec{u} \vert &= \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2} \\
\vert \vec{v} \vert &= \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the arccosine formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{joint}} &= \arccos\!\left( \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{\vert \vec{u} \vert \cdot \vert \vec{v} \vert} \right) \quad \text{[radians]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* When the two bones are straight (fully extended finger), the vectors are parallel, the dot product equals the product of magnitudes, and $\theta = 0$. When the finger is fully curled, the vectors are anti-parallel and $\theta = \pi$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Gesture Design Decision Table

| Design Decision | Recommended Choice | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gestures in vocabulary | **8 – 12** | Beyond 12, recall drops sharply (Miller's Law) |
| Single-hand vs two-hand | **Single-hand for selection, two-hand for manipulation** | Two-handed scaling/rotation is more intuitive |
| Static vs dynamic gestures | **Mix both** | Static = pose, dynamic = motion path |
| Feedback modality | **Visual + haptic** | Visual is primary, haptic confirms in low-attention tasks |
| Recognition technique | **ML (Random Forest / CNN / LSTM)** | Rule-based is brittle, ML scales |
| Sensor type for AR/VR | **Inside-out RGB-D cameras + IMU** | Standard in Meta Quest, HoloLens |
| Onboarding | **Interactive tutorial with guided hand silhouette** | Reduces abandonment rate by ~40% |

## 3.4 Implementation Walkthrough — Building a Gesture-Controlled AR App (5 Steps)

**Step 1: Define the gesture vocabulary**

List the gestures the app will support. Example for an AR shopping app:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vocabulary} = \{ & \text{SwipeRight (next product)}, \\
                        & \text{SwipeLeft (previous product)}, \\
                        & \text{Pinch (zoom in)}, \\
                        & \text{OpenPalm (reset view)}, \\
                        & \text{ThumbsUp (add to cart)} \} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Choose a hand-tracking SDK**

For ARCore (Android) / ARKit (iOS) / Unity XR / Unreal: select from MediaPipe Hands, OpenXR hand-tracking extension, Meta Interaction SDK, or Ultraleap.

**Step 3: Capture and preprocess**

Acquire hand keypoints at $\geq 30$ Hz. Apply a low-pass filter (e.g., exponential moving average) to reduce jitter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{smooth}}[t] &= \alpha \cdot P_{\text{raw}}[t] + (1 - \alpha) \cdot P_{\text{smooth}}[t-1] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

with $\alpha \approx 0.3$ for AR/VR (lower = smoother, higher = more responsive).

**Step 4: Classify**

Feed the time-series of keypoints to a classifier. A simple LSTM network works well:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_t &= \text{LSTM}(x_t, h_{t-1}) \\
y_t &= \text{softmax}(W_y h_t + b_y) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $x_t$ is the keypoint vector at time $t$ and $y_t$ is the predicted gesture probability distribution.

**Step 5: Map and render feedback**

Map the recognized gesture to a Unity or Unreal event. Provide a visual "hand cursor" and optionally a haptic pulse (e.g., 100 ms vibration on the controller).

## 3.5 The Perceived Latency Equation — Derivation

Total user-perceived latency is the sum of three terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{perceived}} &= L_{\text{sense}} + L_{\text{compute}} + L_{\text{render}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For an AR/VR gesture system, the KTU-accepted budget is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{sense}} &\le 20 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(camera exposure + readout)} \\
L_{\text{compute}} &\le 30 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(preprocessing + classification)} \\
L_{\text{render}} &\le 20 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(display refresh at 60 Hz)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{perceived}} &\le 20 + 30 + 20 = 70 \text{ ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Exceeding 70 ms is the **single most cited reason** for "VR sickness" in user studies. The KTU board expects this 70 ms threshold in any answer that mentions AR/VR comfort.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Gesture Interaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Body Movement] --> B[Sensor Array]
    B --> C[Preprocessing Module]
    C --> D[Feature Extractor]
    D --> E[Gesture Classifier]
    E --> F[Action Mapper]
    F --> G[AR/VR Scene Update]
    G --> H[Feedback Renderer]
    H --> I[Haptic Output]
    H --> J[Visual Output]
    H --> K[Audio Output]
    H --> A
```

*Explanation of the closed loop:*
- The **user moves** (A) and the **sensor array** (B) — typically RGB-D cameras + IMU — captures raw data.
- The **preprocessing module** (C) removes noise and normalizes the data.
- The **feature extractor** (D) reduces the data to keypoints, joint angles, and velocity.
- The **gesture classifier** (E) assigns a label (e.g., "SwipeRight").
- The **action mapper** (F) translates the label to a scene command.
- The **scene update** (G) modifies the AR/VR world.
- The **feedback renderer** (H) closes the loop by sending outputs to the **haptic** (I), **visual** (J), and **audio** (K) channels, which the user perceives and reacts to.

## 4.2 Gesture Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Hand Pose Vector] --> P1{Is Hand Open?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2{Check Wrist Velocity}
```
